1: Jabin king of Hazor triggers the northern coalition. Hazor is identified in v.10 as 'the head of all those kingdoms' — the dominant city-state of the north, confirmed by archaeology (Tell el-Qedah, the largest Bronze Age site in Canaan, ~200 acres). Jabin's name recurs in Judges 4 — either a dynastic name or a second Jabin after Israel's withdrawal. The name yābîn means 'he understands/discerns'; the name signals intelligence in the ruler, not just martial force.
2: The geographic spread — northern hill country, Arabah south of Kinnereth (Sea of Galilee), the western foothills, and the heights of Dor — covers the entire northern Levant. The campaign will be qualitatively different from the south: larger distances, different terrain, and a cavalry-heavy enemy (v.4).
3: The coalition includes Hivites (ḥiwwî, here specifically at Mizpah) — the same people group as the Gibeonites. The enumeration of peoples (Canaanites east/west, Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Jebusites) mirrors the standard Deuteronomy list (Deut 7:1) of the seven nations to be dispossessed. The totality of the opposition emphasizes YHWH's total victory.
4: wayyēṣĕʾû hēm wĕkol-maḥănêhem ʿimmāhem ʿam rab kaḥôl ʾašer ʿal-śĕpat hayyām lārōb — 'they came out, they and all their forces with them, a vast people, as the sand on the shore of the sea.' The sand simile (kaḥôl ʾašer ʿal-śĕpat hayyām) is the standard biblical idiom for an uncountable multitude (Gen 22:17; Judg 7:12; 1 Sam 13:5). The force includes cavalry (sûs wārekem) and chariots — weaponry Israel lacks and has been told not to desire (v.6).
5: Assembly at the Waters of Merom — likely modern Meiron in Upper Galilee, a natural assembly point in the hill country. The coalition chooses defensible terrain; YHWH instructs a surprise attack (v.7) that neutralizes the positional advantage.
6: wayyōʾmer YHWH ʾel-yĕhôšuaʿ ʾal-tîrāʾ mippĕnêhem — 'YHWH said to Joshua, do not fear them.' The reassurance is necessary because the northern coalition (chariots, horses, vast numbers) represents a military capability far beyond anything Israel has faced. The specific command: ʾet-sûsêhem tĕʿaqqēr wĕʾet-merkĕbōtêhem tiśrōp bāʾēš — 'you shall hamstring their horses and burn their chariots.' This deliberately deprives Israel of captured military technology; they must not build their security on horses and chariots (Ps 20:7; Deut 17:16). The command enforces dependence on YHWH rather than military parity.
7: wayyābōʾ yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkol-ʿam hammiḥāmāh ʿimmô ʾălêhem ʿal-mê mērôm pitʾōm — 'Joshua and all the people of war with him fell upon them at the Waters of Merom suddenly.' Pitʾōm (suddenly) is the same tactical surprise used in ch.10:9. The surprise assault disrupts the enemy's formation before chariots and cavalry can be deployed — YHWH's strategy compensates for Israel's technological disadvantage.
8: YHWH gave them into Israel's hand (wayyittēm YHWH bĕyad-yiśrāʾēl). The divine passive precedes the tactical pursuit. The pursuit extends in four directions — Sidon (northwest coast), Misrephoth-maim (northern Phoenicia), the Valley of Mizpah (east) — showing that the coalition scattered in all directions, indicating a rout rather than a fighting retreat.
9: wayyaʿas lāhem yĕhôšuaʿ kaʾašer ʾāmar lô YHWH ʾet-sûsêhem ʿiqqēr wĕʾet-merkĕbōtêhem śārap bāʾēš — 'Joshua did to them as YHWH had said: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots.' Exact obedience to the divine command, even when it meant destroying valuable military assets. The verse establishes the pattern for all of ch.11: YHWH commanded → Joshua obeyed completely (v.15: 'he left nothing undone').
10: wayyāšob yĕhôšuaʿ bāʿēt hahîʾ wayyilkōd ʾet-ḥāṣôr wayyak ʾet-malkāh beḥāreb — 'Joshua turned at that time, captured Hazor, and struck its king with the sword.' Hazor is identified as rōʾš kol-hammamlākôt hāhēnnāh — 'head of all those kingdoms.' Its special destruction (burning, v.11) sets it apart from other northern cities; it is the northern equivalent of Jericho — the symbolic capital whose fall represents the collapse of the whole region.
11: wayyakû ʾet-kol-hannepeš ʾašer-bāh lĕpî-ḥereb haḥărēm ʾōtāh lōʾ niśʾar kol-nĕšāmāh — 'they struck every person in it with the sword, devoting it to destruction; not a single living thing was left.' The ḥērem formula at full intensity; nĕšāmāh (breath/living thing) is used alongside nepeš (person) for emphasis. Hazor alone is burned (wĕʾet-ḥāṣôr śārap bāʾēš) — the same fate as Jericho (Josh 6:24), Ai (8:28), and the chariots (v.9). Archaeological layers at Tell el-Qedah confirm a major destruction in the Late Bronze Age.
12: All the cities of the northern kings were taken. wayyak kol-malkām lĕpî-ḥereb haḥărēm ʾōtām — 'he struck all their kings with the sword, devoting them to destruction.' The obedience formula: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh mōšeh ʿebed YHWH — 'as Moses the servant of YHWH had commanded.' The Mosaic mandate (Deut 7:1-5; 20:16-18) is the explicit grounding for the ḥērem; Joshua acts under Mosaic authority, not personal initiative.
13: Cities standing on their mounds (ʿārîm hāʿōmĕdôt ʿal-tillām) were not burned by Israel — only Hazor among the north is burned. This observation has archaeological significance: it explains why most northern sites show continuity layers rather than destruction layers in the relevant strata. The 'tells' (mound-cities) were preserved for Israelite habitation; only Hazor, as the symbolic capital, was destroyed.
14: wĕkol-šĕlal hehārîm hāʾēlleh wĕhabbĕhēmāh bāzĕzû lāhem bĕnê yiśrāʾēl — 'all the spoil of these cities and the livestock, Israel plundered for themselves.' The distinction is explicit: livestock and goods may be taken, but the ḥērem applies to persons. This matches the Deuteronomic law (Deut 20:14) for distant cities, even though the northern campaign was against cities that fell under the total-destruction command.
15: kaʾašer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh ʿabdô kēn ṣiwwāh mōšeh ʾet-yĕhôšuaʿ wĕkēn ʿāśāh yĕhôšuaʿ lōʾ-hēsîr dābār mikkōl ʾašer-ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet-mōšeh — 'as YHWH commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua, and so Joshua did — he left nothing undone of all that YHWH commanded Moses.' This is the book's highest obedience formula: a three-link chain (YHWH→Moses→Joshua) with the explicit affirmation that nothing was omitted. This verse anchors the entire conquest as an act of covenantal obedience, not aggressive expansion.
16: The geographic summary of ch.11's conquest echoes the southern summary of ch.10:40-41, now extended north. The full schema: hill country, Negev, Goshen, the lowlands, the Arabah, hill country and foothills of Israel. The idealized totality ('all this land Joshua took') is the summary perspective; chs.13-19 will detail what remains.
17: From Mount Halak (southern boundary) to Baal-gad in the Lebanon Valley (northern boundary) — the full north-south axis of the Promised Land. The formula kol-malkêhem lāqaḥ wayyakkēm wayyĕmîtēm — 'he took all their kings, struck them, and put them to death' — applies to the entire campaign arc. The comprehensive victory claim is the narrator's theological assessment: YHWH gave what he promised.
18: yāmîm rabbîm ʿāśāh yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hamĕlākîm hāʾēlleh miḥāmāh — 'Joshua waged war against all these kings for a long time.' This verse is often overlooked but is crucial: the conquest was not rapid or magical. Yāmîm rabbîm ('many days') indicates years of ongoing warfare — archaeology supports a prolonged process, not a seven-year blitzkrieg. The theological point stands regardless: YHWH's faithfulness does not always mean immediate completion.
19: lōʾ hāyĕtāh ʿîr ʾašer hiśslîmāh ʾel-bĕnê yiśrāʾēl biltî haḥiwwî yōšĕbê gibʿôn — 'there was not a city that made peace with Israel, except the Hivites who lived in Gibeon.' The Gibeonite exception is the only successful treaty; every other city chose war and faced destruction. The Gibeonites' survival is explicitly grounded in their unique decision for peace — a note that has canonical weight: the door was open, but only one people walked through it.
20: kî mēʾēt YHWH hāyĕtāh lĕḥazzēq ʾet-libbām liqraʾt hammiḥāmāh ʾet-yiśrāʾēl — 'for it was from YHWH that it was to harden their hearts to meet Israel in war.' The ḥizzēq (harden) construction mirrors Exodus Pharaoh texts precisely: in Exodus, YHWH hardens Pharaoh's heart after Pharaoh first hardens his own; here the hardening is YHWH's initiative applied to the Canaanite kings. The purpose: lemaʿan haḥărîmām — 'in order to devote them to destruction.' The Canaanites' resistance is not presented as unfortunate obstruction but as divinely ordered — they chose war, and YHWH confirmed that choice so the judgment would be complete.
21: The Anakites (ʿănāqîm) — the giants who terrified the spies in Num 13:33 ('we seemed like grasshoppers to ourselves'). Their elimination from Israelite territory is presented as the resolution of Numbers' crisis: what the first generation feared and could not conquer, Joshua's generation destroys. The survivors are confined to Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod — Philistine cities. The Goliath of 1 Sam 17 (gat) is the Anakite remnant the next generation will face; David will finish what Joshua started.
22: lōʾ-nôtar ʿănāqîm bĕʾereṣ bĕnê yiśrāʾēl raq bĕʿazzāh bĕgat ûbĕʾašdôd niśʾārû — 'no Anakites were left in Israelite territory; only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod did any remain.' The specificity of Gaza, Gath, Ashdod as Philistine cities is prospective: these are not just geographical notes but narrative setup for the Philistine conflicts and Goliath (1 Sam 17:4 — 'a Philistine... from Gath'). The seed of the next crisis is planted in the cleanup statement of the previous victory.
23: wayyiqqaḥ yĕhôšuaʿ ʾet-kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕkōl ʾašer dibber YHWH ʾel-mōšeh — 'Joshua took the entire land, according to all that YHWH had spoken to Moses.' This verse closes both ch.11 and the conquest narrative (chs.1-11). Three affirmations: (1) the land was taken completely; (2) it matches YHWH's word to Moses (Gen 15:18-21 through Deut 34); (3) wayyanhilāh lĕyiśrāʾēl kĕmahălĕqōtām lĕšibṭêhem — 'he gave it as inheritance to Israel according to their tribal divisions.' The word naḥălāh (inheritance, portion, allotment) is the key term of the land theology; it implies permanent gift, not merely military conquest. Wattišqōt hāʾāreṣ mimmilḥāmāh — 'and the land had rest from war.' Šāqaṭ (rest, quietness) is the Deuteronomistic rest-formula (Deut 12:10); the land's rest is the partial fulfilment of the Sabbath-rest promised to Israel, which Hebrews 4:1-11 argues points toward a greater and permanent rest in Christ.
